title: Psalms 60
subtitle: You Have Rejected Us, O God

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — David Captures Jerusalem

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “You Have Shaken the Land and Torn It Open”

heb.paragraph: "You have rejected us (zənaḥtānū), God, and burst upon us; you have been angry — now restore us!" (v.1). A communal lament after military defeat. The land is shaken, torn open (v.2). God made his people drink the wine of staggering (v.3). But then: "you have raised a banner for those who fear you, that they may rally to it" (v.4). Even in defeat, God provides a rallying point.

notes.note: "You have rejected us. You have shaken the land." National disaster attributed directly to God. The defeat is theological, not merely military.
"You have raised a banner for those who fear you." Even in rejection, a rallying banner. God has not abandoned; he has disciplined.
"Save us and help us with your right hand. God has spoken from his sanctuary: ‘In triumph I will parcel out Shechem.’" God’s oracle: the land still belongs to him. He will redistribute it in victory.

refs.note: The communal lament over military defeat uses identical rejection language.
Another communal lament asking 'how long' before God restores his rejected people.
The LORD handing Israel over to raiders follows the same theological pattern of divine rejection in battle.

hist.context: Psalm 60 is attributed to David's wars in Aram and Edom when Joab struck down 12,000 Edomites in the Valley of Salt (2 Sam 8; 1 Chr 18). The superscription references 'teaching' (maskil), suggesting didactic purpose. The earthquake and reeling (vv.2-3) may be metaphorical for national upheaval or reflect an actual earthquake during the campaign.

sections.header: Verses 7–12 — “With God We Will Gain the Victory”

heb.paragraph: "With God we will gain the victory (ḥayil), and he will trample down our enemies" (v.12). God claims territories: Gilead is mine, Manasseh is mine, Ephraim is my helmet, Judah is my sceptre (v.7). Moab is his washbasin, Edom the place to throw his sandal (v.8). "Who will bring me to the fortified city?" (v.9). The psalm’s final confidence: not our army but our God gains the victory.

notes.note: "Gilead is mine, Manasseh is mine, Ephraim my helmet, Judah my sceptre. Moab is my washbasin; on Edom I toss my sandal." God claims every region as his property. The defeated feel abandoned; God says: I own all of it.
"Give us aid against the enemy, for human help is worthless. With God we will gain the victory." The psalm’s thesis: human help fails; divine help triumphs. Same theology as Psalms 20:7 and 33:16–17.

refs.note: This oracle of divine geography (vv.6-12) appears again in Psalm 108 with minor variations.
David's conquest of Moab, Edom, and Philistia fulfills the territorial claims in this psalm.
Obadiah's prophecy of Israel possessing Edom develops the psalm's Edomite conquest theme.

hist.context: The divine oracle (vv.6-8) claims dominion over Shechem, Succoth, Gilead, Manasseh, Ephraim, and Judah, then treats Moab as a washbasin and Edom as a place to toss sandals — gestures of contempt and ownership. The confession that 'human help is worthless' (v.11) reorients trust from military strategy to divine intervention.

ppl.name: God (Elohim)
ppl.role: The warrior-God who both rejects and restores his people (vv.1-5,10-12)
ppl.text: Israel has suffered military defeat and God is identified as the cause: 'You have rejected us, God, and burst upon us; you have been angry — now restore us!' (v.1). God 'made the land tremble and torn it open' (v.2) and gave his people 'wine that makes us stagger' (v.3). Yet the same God who breaks also restores: he raises 'a banner for those who fear you' (v.4) and promises to divide up conquered territories (vv.6-8). The theological tension — God as both wounding and healing — is central to Israel's covenant theology (cf. Deut 32:39, Hos 6:1).

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Military commander processing defeat while trusting God's promise (superscription, vv.5,9-12)
ppl.text: The superscription provides complex historical context: David fought Aram Naharaim and Aram Zobah while Joab defeated Edom in the Valley of Salt (cf. 2 Sam 8:13; 1 Chr 18:12). David's question 'Who will bring me to the fortified city? Who will lead me to Edom?' (v.9) reveals strategic uncertainty — can God's promises survive military reality? The psalm ends with hard-won faith: 'With God we will gain the victory, and he will trample down our enemies' (v.12), a declaration that became Israel's military confession.

ppl.name: Moab, Edom, and Philistia
ppl.role: Nations assigned roles in God's sovereign geography (vv.6-8)
ppl.text: In a striking oracle, God claims territory and assigns humiliating roles: Moab is 'my washbasin,' Edom receives his thrown sandal (a claim of ownership), and Philistia is told to 'shout in triumph' — sarcastically, as a conquered people forced to cheer their conqueror (vv.8). This divine war oracle, spoken in first person, maps Israel's geopolitical landscape onto God's sovereignty. Shechem and Sukkoth (v.6), Gilead and Manasseh (v.7) are parceled out as God's personal property.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVYou have shaken the land and torn it open; mend its fractures, for it is quaking.ESVYou have made the land to quake; you have torn it open; repair its breaches, for it totters.NIVSave us and help us with your right hand, that those you love may be delivered.ESVThat your beloved ones may be delivered, give salvation by your right hand and answer us!

rec.text: ""You have rejected us. You have shaken the land." National disaster attributed directly to God. The defeat is theological, not merely military."

rows.label: National Defeat — 60:1–6
rows.text: "You have rejected us." Land shaken. But a banner raised. God’s oracle of ownership.

hebtext.note: "You have rejected us (zənaḥtānū), God, and burst upon us; you have been angry — now restore us!" (v.1). A communal lament after military defeat. The land is shaken, torn open (v.2). God made his people drink the wine of staggering (v.3). But then: "you have raised a banner for those who fear you, that they may rally to it" (v.4). Even in defeat, God provides a rallying point.

"With God we will gain the victory (ḥayil), and he will trample down our enemies" (v.12). God claims territories: Gilead is mine, Manasseh is mine, Ephraim is my helmet, Judah is my sceptre (v.7). Moab is his washbasin, Edom the place to throw his sandal (v.8). "Who will bring me to the fortified city?" (v.9). The psalm’s final confidence: not our army but our God gains the victory.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: God owns every territory and provides victory. Faith: "human help is worthless." Judgment: defeat as divine discipline.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on 'You have rejected us, O God,' consider asking God whether national or communal suffering has shaken your faith — and whether you can bring corporate grief before Him as honestly as personal grief.
